coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to high-energy products is an effective way to utilize solar energy and mitigate the greenhouse effect. In this paper, a series of CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2/NiAl-LDH (C/BOC/LDH) photocatalysts were prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal method, demonstrated excellent photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance. In the case of only water without any photosensitizer and sacrificial agent, the CO production rate on C/0.3BOC/LDH reached 16.4 μmol·g-1h−1, which is 6.7 times higher than that of the original LDH. The construction of Z-scheme heterojunctions inhibited the recombination of electrons with holes. The unique up-conversion PL behavior of CQDs benefitted the absorption of energy in the NIR by the photocatalyst. This study provides meaningful assistance for the design and construction of a ternary photocatalytic system with Z-scheme heterojunction and carbon-based co-catalyst.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of LDH, BOC monomer, C/LDH, BOC/LDH, and C/BOC/LDH composites.
SEM images of LDH (A, B), Bi12O17Cl2 monomer (C, D), and BOC/LDH composite (E, F).

TEM images of LDH (A), Bi12O17Cl2 (B), and C/0.3BOC/LDH composite (C); HR-TEM images of C/0.3BOC/LDH composite (D); EDX plot of C/0.3BOC/LDH composite (E); and mapping analysis of C/0.3BOC/LDH composite (F).

XPS images of survey spectra (A); high-resolution Ni 2p (B) and Al 2p (C) spectra of LDH and C/0.3BOC/LDH composite; high-resolution Bi 4f (D), Cl 2p (E), and O 1 s (F) spectra of BOC and C/0.3BOC/LDH composite; high-resolution C 1 s spectra of C/0.3BOC/LDH composite.
UV–vis DRS spectra (A), (αhν)1/2 versus energy (hν) for the band gap energies (B) of pure LDH, BOC, C/LDH, BOC/LDH, and C/BOC/LDH.

Photocatalytic CO yields of as-prepared samples under simulated sunlight irradiation (A) and λ > 800 nm light irradiation (B); repeatability of photocatalytic CO evolution via the C/0.3BOC/LDH photocatalyst under simulated sunlight irradiation (C) and λ > 800 nm light irradiation (D).
Spectra of PL (A); TPR under simulated sunlight irradiation (B) and λ > 800 nm light irradiation (C); EIS (D); and time-resolved PL decay curves (E) of as-fabricated photocatalysts.
The Mott-Schottky plots for pure LDH (A) and BOC (B).
Schematic illustration of type-Ⅱ (A) and Z-scheme (B) charge transfer mechanisms during photocatalytic reduction of CO2 on C/BOC/LDH.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-modified Z-scheme Bi12O17Cl2/NiAl-LDH for significantly boosting photocatalytic CO2 reduction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to high-energy products is an effective way to utilize solar energy and mitigate the greenhouse effect. In this paper, a series of CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2/NiAl-LDH (C/BOC/LDH) photocatalysts were prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal method, demonstrated excellent photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance. In the case of only water without any photosensitizer and sacrificial agent, the CO production rate on C/0.3BOC/LDH reached 16.4 μmol·g-1h−1, which is 6.7 times higher than that of the original LDH. The construction of Z-scheme heterojunctions inhibited the recombination of electrons with holes. The unique up-conversion PL behavior of CQDs benefitted the absorption of energy in the NIR by the photocatalyst. This study provides meaningful assistance for the design and construction of a ternary photocatalytic system with Z-scheme heterojunction and carbon-based co-catalyst.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, sustainable development is currently one of the major challenges facing humanity, especially the energy crisis caused by the rapid consumption of fossil energy and the greenhouse effect caused by the massive emission of greenhouse gases [1–3]. Therefore, it is urgent to explore environmentally sustainable energy utilization technologies. Converting carbon dioxide (CO2) into valuable products is undoubtedly an effective way [4–8]. Photocatalytic CO2 reduction can realize the effective utilization of solar energy and convert CO2 into valuable renewable energy such as CO, methane, methanol, ethane, which is a double benefit. Meanwhile, the reaction process has no additional consumption of electrical and thermal energy, is more environmentally friendly and has a broad development prospect [9–14]. However, since CO2 molecules are relatively stable under normal conditions, the reduction reaction is not easy to occur [15]. Therefore, developing efficient and stable catalysts is crucial to achieve high-efficient photocatalytic CO2 conversion. [16].

Layered double hydroxides (LDH), also known as hydrotalcite, has a typical two-dimensional layered structure similar to brucite (Mg(OH)2), consisting of positively charged host layers, interlayer anions, and water molecules [17]. Its chemical formula can be expressed as [M2+ 1-xM3+ x(OH)2]x+(A n
- x/n)·mH2O, thereinto, M2+ represents divalent metal cations, such as Mg2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cu2+; M3+ represents trivalent metal cations, including: Al3+, Fe3+, Co3+, Cr3+, Ga3+, and × is the molar ratio of M3+/(M2+ + M3+), which is generally considered to be 0.2 ≤ x ≤ 0.33 [18–20]. With an adjustability of the composition, adjustable acidity and basicity, well thermal stability as well as excellent memory effect, LDH has a broad application potential as a heterogeneous catalyst [21–23]. Furthermore, since LDH exhibits weak basicity, which is conducive to the adsorption of CO2 molecules [24], it can also be applied in photocatalytic CO2 reduction. Among the numerous LDH materials, CoAl-LDH, NiAl-LDH and MgAl-LDH exhibit excellent performance and stability [25–28]. Wherein, NiAl-LDH exhibits extremely high CO selectivity thanks to the smaller H2 generation ratio and the presence of Ni species [29]. However, the drawbacks of easy recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, insufficient light capturing ability, and weak reduction ability, limit its CO2 conversion efficiency [30]. In recent years, with the continuous in-depth research, LDH-based photocatalysts with different characteristics and functions have been obtained by modulating the composition, morphology, band structure and defects of LDH through doping, intercalation, calcination, reduction, loading and constructing heterojunctions. As reported by Tan et al., abundant metal and hydroxyl defects were generated in monolayer NiAl-LDH, reducing the Gibbs free energy barrier for CO2 reduction to CH4 [31]. Meanwhile, the separation of carriers is also more efficient in monolayer LDH. Ning et al. introduced Co into MgAl-LDH to promote the reaction of CO2 molecules adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst with H+, and inhibit the generation of H2 [32]. Chen et al. coupled Ti3C2TX nanosheets with ultrathin CoCo-LDH nanosheets, taking advantage of the high electrical conductivity of Ti3C2TX to enable fast and efficient electron transport across the hybrid interface [33].

Benefiting from the abundant reserves of Bi element, Bi-based semiconductors are widely used as photocatalysts [34]. Bismuth oxyhalides, BixOyAz (A = F, Cl, Br, I), are a new class of Bi-based photocatalytic materials [35], with the advantages of chemical stability, non-toxicity, and corrosion resistance [36]. Thereinto, Bi12O17Cl2 has shown admirable performance in the degradation of pollutants [37,38], oxidation [39] and so on. Furthermore, its narrow band gap (2.2–2.5 eV) gives rise to excellent visible light absorption while causing the inefficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole, which limits its practical application [39–43]. By constructing heterojunction, electrons and holes are accumulated on the conduction bands (CB) and valence bands (VB) of the two semiconductors, respectively, which is an effective approach to suppress electron-hole recombination. In contrast to type-Ⅱ heterojunction, Z-scheme heterojunction can realize the effective separation of photoexcited carriers without reducing its redox potential. Quan et al. uniformly loaded 0D carbonized polymer dots onto Bi12O17Cl2 nanosheets to construct a Z-scheme heterojunction, effectively improving the carrier separation efficiency, and realizing efficient photocatalytic conversion of CO2 to CO [44]. Huo et al. combined ultrathin Bi12O17Cl2 with porous g-C3N4 and took advantage of the generated built-in electric field to drive carrier transfer, yielding photocatalytic activity superior to that of pure Bi12O17Cl2 and g-C3N4 [45]. Currently, g-C3N4 [46–48] and other Bi-based materials [49–51] are usually used to construct heterojunctions with Bi12O17Cl2, but there are few reports on the photocatalytic CO2 reduction over Bi12O17Cl2/LDH.

Carbon, known as black matter, is one of the most abundant elements in nature, and carbon-based materials have the advantages of high mechanical strength and electrical conductivity [21]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel carbon-based nanomaterial, consists of spherical and discrete nanoparticles. In recent years, CQDs has received increasing attention owing to its high aqueous solubility, low toxicity, excellent luminescence properties, especially unique up-conversion photoluminescence (PL) behavior [52–55]. CQDs has been widely used in sensing [56], optoelectronic devices [57], and drug delivery [58]. Furthermore, CQDs also has good electron transfer and storage characteristics and is considered as a suitable choice for modifying photocatalysts.

In this study, CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2/NiAl-LDH ternary photocatalysts were successfully designed and prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method. The as-manufactured catalyst yielded higher photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity than pure NiAl-LDH and Bi12O17Cl2. Additionally, the reduction reaction mechanism was investigated and discussed based on the characterization results.

Citric acid, urea, BiCl3, ethanol, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (Al(NO3)3·9H2O) and ammonium fluoride (NH4F) were provided by the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Corp, P. R. China. All the above reagents are of analytical reagent (AR) grade and do not require further purification.

CQDs was prepared by a simple hydrothermal method [59,60]. 6.0 g citric acid and 2.0 g urea were solubilized in deionized water and sonicated for 2 h. Afterwards, the resulting mixture was shifted into a 100 mL autoclave and held at 180 ℃ for 5 h before being cooled to ambient temperature to obtain a black solution. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min, and the supernatant was collected and reconstituted in deionized water to a 100 mL solution for storage.

Bi12O17Cl2 was prepared by precipitation [61]. 1.26 g BiCl3 was dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol and ultrasonically dispersed. Then, 12.6 mL of 2.0 M NaOH solution was added drop by drop into the above solution under constant stirring, followed by vigorous stirring for 4 h. The slurry was collected and washed three times with deionized water and dried at 60 ℃ overnight.

Pure NiAl-LDH and the compound were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method. Appropriate amounts of as-fabricated CQDs and Bi12O17Cl2 powder were dispersed in 120 mL of deionized water solution containing 1.745 g Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.750 g Al(NO3)3·9H2O by ultrasonic. Then, 2.4 g urea and 0.592 g NH4F were added into the mixed solution and stirred magnetically for 2 h. The resulting mixture was then transferred into a 150 mL autoclave with Teflon-lined and maintained at 120 ℃ for 24 h. The precipitate was washed 6 times with anhydrous ethanol and deionized water and dried overnight at 80 ℃ under vacuum. C/BOC/LDH photocatalysts were prepared with the same 1.0 wt% of CQDs. C/BOC/LDH samples with different Bi12O17Cl2 mass ratios of 20, 30, and 40 wt% were labeled as C/0.2BOC/LDH, C/0.3BOC/LDH, and C/0.4BOC/LDH, respectively. Pure LDH and Bi12O17Cl2/NiAl-LDH (denoted as BOC/LDH) were also prepared using the same method.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra was acquired on a Bruker D8 Advance powder diffractometer (Cu Kα radiation, λ = 0.154 nm) to identify the crystalline structures of the catalyst samples. The morphology of the prepared catalysts was observed by the scanning electron microscope (SEM, Phillips XL-30 FEG/NEW) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, Phillips Model CM200). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with Al Kα radiation sources was used to clarify the states of the chemical elements. The UV–vis diffuse reflection spectrum (UV–vis DRS) in the range of 200–2500 nm was documented on a spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU UV-3600, Japan) to characterize the optical absorption properties of the samples. Photoluminescence (PL) properties were studied on a spectrofluorometer (Hitachi F-4600) with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. The reactive radicals during the reduction reaction were analyzed by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, JES FA200).

The photoelectrochemical test of photocatalyst is the same as our previous study [62]. Electrochemical measurement and analysis were carried out by an electrochemical device (CHI 660E, Shanghai Chenhua, China). A 300 W Xenon arc lamp and a 3 AM 1.5 G filter were used to simulate sunlight, and the photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity was evaluated on a Labsolar-6A system (Perfect Light Co., China). 50 mg of photocatalyst was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water, and the obtained suspension was continuously stirred for 30 min in a vacuum. Afterward, the suspension was saturated with pure CO2 at 100 kPa for 30 min to realize an adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Then, the lamp was turned on for 7 consecutive hours. In addition, the system was maintained at 5 ℃ throughout the catalytic reaction. The reactor was connected to a gas chromatograph (GC-2010 Plus, SHIMADZU, Japan) to analyze the composition of the generated gases. H2 and O2 in the mixture were identified by a thermal conductivity detector (TCD), and CO was analyzed using a flame ionization detector (FID). Isotope labelling test was carried out on GCMS-QP2010 (SHIMADZU, Japan) using 13CO2 as the only input gas to determine the carbon source in the product.

The apparent quantum yield (AQY) of the photocatalyst was estimated using Eq. (1), as reported in the previous study [63]. The power of the light source used was 300 W, the light intensity was 100 mW cm−2, the wavelength was 420 nm, and the volume of the reactor was 250 mL with 12 cm−2 of incident area.
(1)
AQY (\%) =
the number of reacted electrons
the number of incident photons
× 100 % =
the number of evolved CO molecules

2
the number of incident photons

The XRD spectra of the prepared samples were used to analyze their crystal structure and phase purity. As indicated in Fig. 1 , the successful preparation of the C/BOC/LDH composite catalyst is clearly demonstrated. The diffraction peaks of pure NiAl-LDH at 11.3, 22.8, 34.6, 39.0, 46.2, 60.2, and 61.5° belong to (003), (006), (012), (015), (018), (110), and (113) planes, respectively [64], complies with the (JCPDS 22–0452). Analogously, the prepared Bi12O17Cl2 presents seven main peaks at 2θ = 23.2, 24.3, 26.4, 30,1, 32.9, 47.2, and 56.5° corresponding to the (111), (113), (115), (117), (200), (220), and (317) planes of tetragonal Bi12O17Cl2, respectively (JCPDS 37–0702) [65]. The needle-like diffraction peaks of both NiAl-LDH and Bi12O17Cl2 show that they have good crystallinity. All the diffraction peaks of LDH and Bi12O17Cl2 are presented in the XRD patterns of 0.3BOC/LDH and C/0.3BOC/LDH composites, indicating the good recombination of LDH and Bi12O17Cl2. Moreover, as the content of CQDs is so low that the resulting diffraction peaks are negligible, the diffraction peaks of 0.01C/LDH and C/0.3BOC/LDH are similar to those of pure LDH and 0.3BOC/LDH, respectively.

The morphology and microstructure of the as-fabricated photocatalysts were appraised by SEM and TEM. As observed, the SEM image of LDH monomer (Fig. 2 (A)) exhibits a flower-like structure with a large number of ultrathin nanosheets. The incident light is reflected repeatedly in the nano-flower ball structure, which minimizes the loss of visible light [66]. Besides, the surface of the nanowires that make up LDH is relatively smooth (Fig. 2(B)). As shown in Fig. 2(C, D), bare Bi12O17Cl2 exhibits the typical 2D nanosheet morphology. In the complex, the original flower-like structure of LDH is maintained and the surface becomes rough, illustrating the loading of Bi12O17Cl2 on it (Fig. 2(E, F)). This confirms the successful preparation of BOC/LDH complex by hydrothermal method.

The surface chemical compositions and chemical states of pure LDH, pure Bi12O17Cl2 and C/0.3BOC/LDH heterostructure were further investigated by XPS. The survey scan XPS spectrum exhibits the existence of Ni, Al, Bi, Cl, O, and C in C/0.3BOC/LDH composite (Fig. 4 (A)). As exhibited in Fig. 4(B), the peaks of Ni 2p are positioned at 873.2 and 855.6 eV, which are attributed to Ni 2p1/2 and Ni 2p3/2, separately [63], which are assigned to the Ni2+ species. Furthermore, the subsidiary satellite peaks at about 879.4 and 861.6 eV also demonstrate the existence of Ni in the high spin-divalent state in the material [13]. For pure LDH, the oxidation states of Al3+ can be defined as the binding energy at 73.8 and 67.9 eV (Fig. 4(C)) [30]. Two sub-bands at 164.2 and 158.5 eV can be observed in Bi 4f spectra of BOC (Fig. 4(D)), which is in correspondence to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 of characteristic Bi3+, separately [37]. The high-resolution Cl 2p spectra is illustrated in Fig. 4(E), where the prominent peaks at 199.6 and 198.0 eV are allocated to Cl 2p1/2 and Cl 2p3/2 of Cl- [65]. Fig. 4(F) displays the O 1 s spectra of BOC and C/0.3BOC/LDH composite. The dominated peak at 529.7 eV belongs to Bi-O bonds [38]. The XPS spectra of C/0.3BOC/LDH in the C 1 s region shows two peaks at 248.8 and 288.9 eV (Fig. 4(G)), which are in tune with the sp 2 hybridized carbon (CC) and carboxy carbon (O-CO), separately [70], further support for the presence of CQDs in C/0.3BOC/LDH. Furthermore, no peaks of Ni-C, Al-C, or Bi-C are observed in the C 1 s spectra, suggesting that CQDs exist as a co-catalyst rather than a dopant in the composite. For C/0.3BOC/LDH composite, the binding energies of Ni 2p and Al 2p are slightly shifted to the low region, while that of Bi 4f, Cl 2p and O 1 s are slightly augmented. These changes in binding energy indicate electrons transfer from Bi12O17Cl2 to NiAl-LDH, further demonstrating the formation of a heterojunction between Bi12O17Cl2 and NiAl-LDH rather than a simple physical mixing.

The optical properties of pure LDH, BOC, 0.3BOC/LDH, 0.01C/LDH and C/0.3BOC/LDH composite photocatalysts were studied by UV–vis DRS in the range of 200–1000 nm. As shown in Fig. 5 (A), pristine BOC presents an intense absorption edge at about 520 nm. While LDH monomer displays three prominent absorption bands in the UV–vis region. The first absorption band situated at 200–300 nm is attributed to ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) from the O 2p orbit to the Ni 3d t2g orbit. The other two bands in the visible range (300–500 and 550–800 nm) are owing to the d-d transition arising from Ni2+ in an octahedral field. The absorption bands positioned at 380 and 740 nm owe their origin to spin-allowed transitions 3A2g(F) → 3T1g(P) and 3A2g(F) → 3T1g(F), separately, as a consequence of the d8 configuration of Ni2+ ions in an octahedral field. severally, which are in tune with the characteristics of the d8 configuration of Ni2+ ions [19,63]. When BOC was compounded with LDH, a marginal red-shift in the absorption edge is generated compared to pure LDH and BOC. The 0.3BOC/LDH binary catalyst has the same absorption properties as BOC in the range of 230–340 nm, which makes the composite catalyst exhibit excellent absorption ability in the UV light region. Whereas this binary composite catalyst exhibits distinct LDH characteristics within 370–870 nm, indicating that the coupling of BOC nanosheets has no impact on the structure of LDH, coinciding with the results observed by XRD and TEM. The enhanced absorption intensity of BOC/LDH in the UV–vis range compared to pure LDH contributes to the excitation of more carriers. It is noticeable that neither LDH nor BOC/LDH can absorb NIR light. As opposed to LDH and BOC/LDH, the introduction of CQDs does not significantly enhance their absorption ability in the UV–vis region, but rather produces a significant absorption of NIR light. This may be contributed to the unique up-conversion PL behavior of CQDs, which can convert longer wavelengths light into photoluminescence with a shorter wavelength (λ < 600 nm) [71]. The wider photoresponse range can enable the catalyst to utilize more solar energy and excite more electrons, which is beneficial for enhancing the photocatalytic activity. Besides, the band gap of LDH, BOC and C/0.3BOC/LDH were also assessed based on the Kubelka-Munk formula αhν = A(hν – Eg)n/2 and the plots of (αhν)1/
n versus (hν) [72], where the value of n is determined by its direct or indirect transition catalyst type. In such cases, the value of n for LDH and BOC is 1/2. Thus, the band gaps of pure LDH, BOC and C/0.3BOC/LDH are 2.67, 2.43 and 3.14 eV, respectively (Fig. 5(B)).

The photocatalytic behaviors of the monomers, binary and ternary complexes were reflected by the reduction of CO2 under the stimulation of simulated sunlight. In the absence of any sacrificial agents and photosensitizers, CO is considered to be the exclusive product of the photocatalytic CO2 reduction (Fig. 6 (A)). Nevertheless, H2 as the additional reduction product and O2 as the additional oxidation product are also observed, as a consequence of the splitting of water (Fig. S1). Controlled experiments were also carried out in the absence of light, catalyst or CO2, respectively, which showed that no reduction products are detected (Fig. S2), suggesting that they are all essential for the CO2 photoreduction reaction.

Fig. 6(A) graphically presents the yields of CO for different catalysts under simulated solar light. The photocatalytic activity of pristine LDH is only 2.46 μmol·g-1h−1, which is attributed to the fast recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs due to the narrow band gap. Under the same reaction conditions, no significant CO is observed when the catalyst is BOC, primarily because its CB position is at a lower potential for CO production. The photocatalytic activity of the binary complexes with different loading of CQDs and BOC under simulated sunlight is demonstrated in Fig. S3. In contrast, 0.01C/LDH exhibits higher catalytic activity than the pristine LDH because the CQDs can utilize the energy in the NIR, increasing the photoresponse range of the catalyst (Fig. S3(A)). When BOC and LDH are compounded together, CO yield increases, suggesting that the heterojunction structure plays an important role in the CO2 reduction. The close contact between BOC nanosheets and flower sphere LDH also facilitates the transmission of electrons. The yield of CO increases first and then decreases as the BOC content increases (Fig. S3(B)). Thanks to the constructed Z-scheme heterojunction and the unique up-conversion PL behavior of CQDs, C/BOC/LDH exhibits a catalytic activity superior to that of pure LDH, BOC and the binary complexes. The optimum CO yield of 16.4 μmol·g-1h−1 is achieved on C/0.3BOC/LDH, which is roughly 6.7 times as much as that of the pristine LDH. Further increase of BOC is detrimental to the improvement of catalytic performance, possibly due to the excessive BOC occupying the reaction sites on LDH or hindering the adsorption of CO2. In the NIR at λ > 800 nm (Fig. 6(B)), neither 0.3BOC/LDH, pure LDH nor BOC can generate CO due to the inability to be excited by NIR light, and only 0.01C/LDH and C/0.3BOC/LDH can reduce CO2 to CO. Among them, the activity of C/0.3BOC/LDH reaches 2.2 μmol·g-1h−1. Moreover, the AQY value of CO obtained in this study is 0.17% at 420 nm. Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) was used to identify the source of C in the CO generation process. As seen in Fig. S4, when 13CO2 (m/z = 45) was used as the carbon source, 13CO (m/z = 29) was detected after 7 h of reaction on C/0.3BOC/LDH. This result demonstrates that all the CO produced in the reduction process is from CO2. We compared the CO2 reduction activity of the C/BOC/LDH ternary catalyst prepared in this study with other NiAl-LDH-based and Bi12O17Cl2-based photocatalysts tested under similar conditions (Table S1). It is obvious that the designed C/BOC/LDH ternary Z-scheme heterojunction-co-catalyst system in our work behave with competitive performance.

As is well known, in addition to the activity of the photocatalyst, stability is also a critical factor in determining its practical application prospect. In order to identify the stability of C/0.3BOC/LDH, the test was cycled three times under the same conditions at full wavelength and λ > 800 nm light, respectively. The yield of CO over C/0.3BOC/LDH remained essentially unchanged after 21 h of sequential light exposure (Fig. 6(C, D)), demonstrating the good stability of the catalyst.

The separation effect of the photoexcited carriers is an important indicator of the photocatalytic activity. Thus, PL spectra were used to investigate the separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers, as shown in Fig. 7 (A). Obviously, the intensities of peaks of the composites are weaker than that of the original LDH, following the sequence of 0.01C/LDH > 0.3BOC/LDH > C/0.3BOC/LDH. The weakest PL intensity of C/0.3BOC/LDH can be explained by the construction of heterojunction and the fact that CQDs loaded on the surface of LDH can act as electron-receivers to capture photogenerated electrons from LDH, thus enabling efficient separation of photogenerated electrons and holes [69]. In addition, thanks to their excellent electron storage capacity, CQDs can serve as active sites for CO2 conversion.

To further elucidate the separation and migration properties of photogenerated carriers in the prepared catalysts, transient photocurrent reaction (TPR) was performed in several switching cycles. In general, the higher the intensity of the photocurrent, the more electrons and holes are available in the redox reaction. In Fig. 7(B), it can be seen that 0.3BOC/LDH produces a stronger photocurrent than pure LDH and BOC under Xe light illumination, indicating that the formed heterojunction can achieve efficient carrier separation. The loading of CQDs onto 0.3BOC/LDH results in a further increase in the intensity of the photocurrent response, much higher than that of 0.01C/LDH and 0.3BOC/LDH, indicating that the manufactured ternary heterojunction-co-catalyst system outperform the binary samples in terms of electron transfer. As a further verification that the loading of CQDs could enable the ternary photocatalyst to be excited by NIR light to generate photogenerated electrons, the photocurrent intensities of the samples were probed under λ > 800 nm irradiation (Fig. 7(C)). In the NIR, the photocurrents of the pristine LDH and BOC are barely detectable due to their inability to be excited by light with λ > 800 nm (Fig. 5(A)). In contrast, thanks to the up-conversion property of CQDs, a significant photocurrent response can be observed on C/0.3BOC/LDH. This is in accordance with the photocatalytic CO2 conversion activity mentioned above. This was also verified by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments. As demonstrated in Fig. 7(D), C/0.3BOC/LDH composite shows the slightest electron transfer resistance with an EIS arc diameter smaller than the binary complexes and monomers. Consequently, the constructed CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2/NiAl-LDH can dramatically accelerate the separation and migration of photogenerated carriers, thus enhancing the photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity.

In addition, the lifetimes (τ) of the optically induced carriers were also investigated by time-resolved fluorescence decay spectroscopy. The decay curves of LDH monomer and C/0.3BOC/LDH composite are fitted with a double exponential decay function (Fig. 7(E)), and the fitted data were inserted into the spectrum. The τ could be measured by the following equation [73]:
(2)

where B1 and B2 are the constants derived from fitting the decay curves, and τ1 and τ2 are the fast and slow decay components, separately. The lifetime of LDH and C/0.3BOC/LDH are 4.58 and 1.74 ns, separately. The results mentioned above manifest that the photogenerated carriers of C/0.3BOC/LDH can rapidly transfer, separate, and be captured by active sites to participate in the reduction reaction.

The energy band positions of the LDH and BOC were measured by recording Mott-Schottky plots at 500 and 1000 Hz (Fig. 8 ). The slopes of the curves for both LDH and BOC are positive, suggesting that they are both n-type semiconductors. The flat-band potentials of LDH and BOC identified from the intersection with the X-axis are roughly equal to −0.56 and 0.15 V, respectively, i.e., −0.36 and 0.35 V (vs NHE, pH = 7). The CB potential of an n-type semiconductor is generally considered to be approximately the same as its flat-band potential [74], so the CB positions of LDH and BOC can be considered as −0.56 and 0.15 V, severally. Based on the band gaps obtained by UV-DRS, the VB of LDH and BOC are calculated to be 2.11 and 2.58 V, separately.

In the heterojunction formed by the BOC and LDH, there are two potential ways for transporting photoexcited carriers: type-Ⅱ and Z-scheme. As illustrated in Fig. 9 (A), in type-Ⅱ heterojunction, the photoexcited electrons generated on the CB in LDH move towards the BOC and accumulate on its CB. This contrasts with the XPS results above. Moreover, the CB potential of BOC is lower than the reduction potential of CO2/CO (-0.53 V vs NHE), and therefore no CO is produced. In Z-scheme heterojunction (Fig. 9(B)), the electrons on the CB of BOC move towards the VB of LDH and recombine with the holes under the effect of a built-in electric field. In contrast, the electrons on LDH and the holes on BOC remain motionless, thus achieving the efficient spatial separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Simultaneously, the band position of LDH (-0.56 V) can meet the potential demand for the reduction of CO2 to CO. Therefore, the Z-scheme heterojunction is plausible.

The EPR technique was used to evaluate the production of ·OH and ·O2 – during the reaction, which helps to understand the photocatalytic charge transfer mechanism [30,75]. ·O2 – is generated by injecting electrons into dissolved O2 in methanol, while ·OH evolves based on the electron extraction from OH– in water. Both radicals can combine with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) and are converted into DMPO-·O2 – and DMPO-·OH adducts, respectively, which are stable radicals suitable for EPR detection [76–78]. Under illumination conditions, both LDH and C/0.3BOC/LDH exhibit four signals in the ratio 1:1:1:1 allocated to DMPO-·O2 – adducts (Fig. 10 (A)), indicating that their reduction potentials can satisfy the generation potential of ·O2 – (-0.33 V) [66]. In contrast, no corresponding signal is observed for Bi12O17Cl2 monomer, which is due to the lack of reduction capacity at its CB position (-0.15 V). Meanwhile, four characteristic peaks of DMPO-·OH adducts with the intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 are observed for pure Bi12O17Cl2 and C/0.3BOC/LDH (as shown in Fig. 10(B)). Because the potential required to generate ·OH (2.3 V) is higher than the VB position of LDH (2.11 V), no ·OH is generated on LDH [79]. These results suggest that the reduction and oxidation reactions over the composite catalyst occur on the CB of LDH and the VB of BOC, respectively. Notably, the intensities of both DMPO-·O2 – and DMPO-·OH signals on C/0.3BOC/LDH are stronger than its constituents, implying that the construction of the heterojunction achieves the separation of electrons and holes. More electrons and holes can be involved in the reduction and oxidation reactions, resulting in its enhanced reduction and oxidation capacities. In summary, the Z-scheme mechanism for C/0.3BOC/LDH can be confirmed.

One possible pathway for photocatalytic CO2 conversion over C/BOC/LDH is proposed based on the above study, as shown in Fig. 9(B). Under the action of simulated sunlight, there are two modalities to excite BOC/LDH to generate photogenerated electrons: 1) being directly excited by UV–vis light with high energy; 2) exploiting the up-conversion property of CQDs loaded on the surface of LDH, the long-wavelength light (λ > 800 nm) could be converted into short-wavelength light to further excite the photogenerated electrons. These two excitation modes enable C/BOC/LDH to utilize the energy of sunlight more efficiently. Under the action of the built-in electric field of the heterojunction, electrons and holes accumulate on the CB of the LDH and the VB of the BOC, respectively. Subsequently, photogenerated electrons are trapped and stored in CQDs. With the exceptional CO2 adsorption capacity generated by the hydroxyl groups on the surface of NiAl-LDH, the concentration of CO2 near the CQDs is increased, promoting CO2 molecules to capture electrons and undergo reduction reactions [80,81]. The photocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction over C/0.3BOC/LDH ternary photocatalysts follows the typical carbene pathway, i.e., CO2 → CO → ·C → ·CH3 → CH3OH/CH4 [82,83]. During this reaction, the product selectivity is mainly determined by the binding energy between CO* and the catalyst. If the binding energy is strong, it helps CO* to combine with e- and h+ and undergo dehydration and hydrogenation processes to obtain the important ·CH3 intermediate, which finally combines with hydroxyl radical or protons to form CH3OH or CH4. Otherwise, the CO* intermediate will be released from the catalyst surface to generate CO. Carbon materials CQDs, as reactive active sites, has weak binding energy to CO*, which is not conducive to the consecutive reactions for the conversion of CO* to CH3OH or CH4 [84,85] . In addition, the H2 evolution reaction (HER) is more accessible in thermodynamics and kinetics and acts as a competition reaction, limiting the formation of hydrocarbon products. In summary, no production of CH3OH and CH4 was observed in this study. The possible electrons transfer process during the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 over C/0.3BOC/LDH Z-scheme heterojunction-co-catalyst system could be depicted by the following equations:
(3) NiAl-LDH + Bi12O17Cl2 + hν → NiAl-LDH(e- + h+) + Bi12O17Cl2(e- + h+)
(4) NiAl-LDH + Bi12O17Cl2(e-) → NiAl-LDH(e-) + Bi12O17Cl2
(5) NiAl-LDH(e-) + CQDs → NiAl-LDH + CQDs(e-)
(6) 2H2O + 4 h+ → O2 + 4H+
(7) CO2 + 2H+ + 2e- → CO + H2O
(8) 2H+ + 2e- → H2

To sum up, CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2/NiAl-LDH ternary photocatalyst was successfully constructed by a one-step hydrothermal method and applied to the conversion of CO2 to CO under simulated sunlight. The prepared C/0.3BOC/LDH realized the highest photocatalytic activity of 16.4 μmol·g-1h−1, which is 6.7 times the CO yield over LDH monomer and exhibited good stability. The excellent performance of the ternary catalyst is attributed to: i) The construction of Z-scheme heterojunction between LDH and BOC achieves the spatial separation of electrons and holes without reducing the redox ability. ii) The introduction of CQDs not only facilitates the separation and migration of photogenerated carriers, but its unique up-conversion PL property enables the catalyst to exploit the energy in the NIR, which expands the photoresponse range and excites more electrons. This study highlights the advantages of CQDs-modified Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst and provides some meaningful assistance in exploring LDH-based catalysts with excellent catalytic activity and a wide photoresponse range.
